docs.title: Circular Economy Ontology Network (CEON)
docs.text:
Welcome to the CEON development documentation site — the companion resource to the CEON ontology catalogue.

This site documents the development process, requirements, competency questions, and SPARQL queries for the Circular Economy Ontology Network. For the ontology modules themselves (serializations, alignments, auto-generated docs), see the main catalogue.

docs.title: What is CEON?
docs.text: The Circular Economy Ontology Network (CEON) provides a shared vocabulary for representing information about circular economies in the form of a network of ontology modules. It is developed by the Linköping University Semantic Web Group as part of the Onto-DESIDE project, funded by the European Union under Horizon Europe Grant Agreement No. 101058682.

docs.title: What's in this documentation?
docs.text:
Section Description Development Process Methodology, design decisions, and versioning strategy Requirements Functional requirements per ontology module Competency Questions CQs used to validate each module SPARQL Queries Ready-to-use SPARQL queries with examples

New to CEON?

Start with the Development Process to understand the design philosophy, then explore Competency Questions to see what kinds of queries the ontology is designed to answer.

docs.title: Development/Maintenance
docs.text: This section describes how CEON was developed and the maintenance plan for CEON.

docs.title: Competency Questions
docs.text: Competency Questions (CQs) are natural language questions that an ontology must be capable of answering. They serve as the primary validation criterion for each CEON module.

docs.title: Circular Enablers Category
docs.text:
• Circular Enabler Category 1
  • Circular Enabler Category 2
  • Circular Enabler Category 3
  • Circular Enabler Category 4
  • Circular Enabler Category 5

docs.title: CQ-CE-1-1
docs.text:
What are the connections and dependencies between actors and processes in a certain value network? (covered)

  • Requirement: CE1
  • SPARQL: CQ-CE-1-1.sparql

docs.title: CQ-CE-1-2
docs.text:
What is the energy components in this system, e.g. exergy and anergy? (covered)

  • Requirement: CE1
  • SPARQL: CQ-CE-1-2.sparql

docs.title: CQ-CE-1-3
docs.text:
What are the involved value forms, e.g. economic, social, environmental? (covered)

  • Requirement: CE1
  • SPARQL: CQ-CE-1-3.sparql

docs.title: CQ-CE-2-1
docs.text:
What are the actors (and their roles) in the value network? (covered)

  • Requirement: CE2
  • SPARQL: CQ-CE-2-1.sparql

docs.title: CQ-CE-2-2
docs.text:
What are the connections and dependencies between actors and materials/components/products in a certain value network? (covered)

  • Requirement: CE2
  • SPARQL: CQ-CE-2-2.sparql (Same as CQ-CE-1-1)

docs.title: CQ-CE-3-1
docs.text:
What is the value network implementing in terms of circular strategies? (covered)

  • SPARQL: CQ-CE-3-1.sparql

docs.title: CQ-CE-3-2
docs.text:
What is the process breakdown of this life cycle (what processes are involved and in what order), across actors? (covered)

  • Requirement: CE3
  • SPARQL: CQ-CE-3-2.sparql

docs.title: CQ-CE-3-3
docs.text:
What is the status and location of this material at this point in time? (partly covered)

  • SPARQL: CQ-CE-3-3.sparql

docs.title: CQ-CE-3-4
docs.text:
What are the elements (e.g. steps/work and actors) involved in this process? (partly covered)

  • SPARQL: CQ-CE-3-4.sparql

docs.title: CQ-CE-3-5
docs.text:
What is the status and location of this material at this point in time? (covered)

  • Requirement: CE5
  • SPARQL: CQ-CE-3-5.sparql

docs.text:
What are the related circular strategies of this and other related value networks? (partly covered)

docs.title: CQ-CE-4-2
docs.text:
What are the connections and dependencies between actors and materials/components/products in a certain value network? (covered)

docs.title: CQ-CE-4-3
docs.text:

docs.text:
What are the elements (e.g. steps/work and actors) involved in this process? (partly covered)

docs.title: CQ-CE-5-2

docs.title: CQ-CE-5-5

docs.title: CQ-CE-5-6

docs.title: CQ-CE-5-7
docs.text:
What are the elements (e.g. steps/work and actors) involved in this process? (partly covered)

docs.title: CQ-CE-5-8

docs.text:

docs.title: CQ-CE-5-11

docs.title: CQ-CE-5-12

docs.title: CQ-CE-5-14
docs.text: What effects would it have on external social and environmental factors? (not covered)

docs.title: CQ-CE-5-15
docs.text:

  • SPARQL: CQ-CE-5-15.sparql

docs.title: CQ-CE-5-16
docs.text:

docs.title: CQ-CE-5-17
docs.text:

docs.title: CQ-CE-5-18

docs.title: CQ-CE-9-2

docs.title: CQ-CE-9-3

docs.title: CQ-CE-9-4

docs.title: CQ-CE-9-5

docs.title: CQ-CE-10-1

docs.title: CQ-CE-10-2

docs.title: CQ-CE-10-4

docs.title: CQ-CE-10-5

docs.title: CQ-CE-10-6

docs.text: What capacity (e.g. competence) is needed to ingrate an acor in the network? (not covered)

docs.title: CQ-CE-11-2

What information does a certain actor hold, or have access to? (partly covered)

docs.text: Who/what actor is responsible for this information? (not covered)

docs.text: Who has accessed this information? (not covered)

docs.title: CQ-CE-13-5
docs.text: What standard/metadata stardard/format is used for the material data? (not covered)

docs.title: CQ-CE-13-6
docs.text: How was this created value verified/is it verified? (not covered)

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text: This section lists the functional requirements for CEON that are extracted from the overview Onto-DESIDE project requirements. These requirements serve as the basis for the Competency Questions.

docs.title: How Requirements Are Organized
docs.text:
Each requirement is represented in two perspectives. One is Circular Enabler Category (CEC), the other is Circular Enablers (sotry), which has a unique identifier (CE<NUMBER>).

Field Description ID Unique identifier, e.g. CE1 Description What the ontology must be able to represent CQs Which competency questions this requirement motivates Reasoning Requirements what corresponding reasoning requirements are

docs.title: Circular Enablers Category
docs.text:
• Circular Enabler Category 1
  • Circular Enabler Category 2
  • Circular Enabler Category 3
  • Circular Enabler Category 4
  • Circular Enabler Category 5

docs.title: Circular Enabler Category 1 – The capacity to understand the system and its relations
docs.text: This caregory includes 4 specific stories and 14 CQs.

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text: ID Description CQs Reasoning Requirements CE1 The capacity to understand interrelations between processes and actors in the system. Implementation actions: The ability to understand all parts of energy (i.e., exergy and anergy). The ability to consider a diverse variety of value forms (incl. economic, environmental, and social). CQ-CE-1-1, CQ-CE-1-2, CQ-CE-1-3 Participation in a value network can be derived from actions and process participation, i.e. collaborations may be implicit. Value and energy may be derived from more detailed processes. CE2 The capacity to identify and consider all (relevant) system actors. Implementation actions: The ability to identify connections by analysing (large amounts of) supply chain data. CQ-CE-2-1, CQ-CE-2-2 Participation in a value network can be derived from actions and process participation, i.e. collaborations may be implicit. CE3 The capacity to consider processes throughout entire life cycle. Implementation actions: The ability to collect data along entire supply chain. The ability to observe and track materials (in real time) throughout all life cycle phases. The ability to collect and analyse large amount of data fast. The ability to visualise and simulate all processes. CQ-CE-3-1, CQ-CE-3-2, CQ-CE-3-3, CQ-CE-3-4, CQ-CE-3-5 Overall processes and strategies may be derived from detailed process data. CE4 The capacity to understand interrelations with other systems (at different levels). Implementation actions: The ability to identify connections by analysing (large amounts of) supply chain data. The ability to understand carbon intensity and sustainability of energy sources. The ability to visualise and simulate all processes. CQ-CE-4-1, CQ-CE-4-2, CQ-CE-4-3, CQ-CE-4-4 Connections can be derived from detailed supply chain data. A process can be characterised by its detailed configuration. Carbon intensity and sustainability factors can be to some extent derived from other data, but calculations may be outside the scope of the ontology.

docs.title: Circular Enabler Category 2 – The capacity to evaluate actions & processes
docs.text: This caregory includes 3 specific stories and 30 CQs.

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text: ID Description CQs Reasoning Requirements CE5 The capacity to scope (new) combinations of processes. Implementation actions: The ability to analyse the feasibility of resource exchange. The ability to record material specifications and activities in central and standardised unit. The ability to understand the connection of the quality and quantity of flows. The ability to incorporate data from various sources. The ability to visually capture processes. The ability to trace materials back to their origin to evaluate energy consumption. The ability to identify energy requirements of rebound effects from material flows. The ability to consider alternatives for achieving efficiency. The ability to forecast energy demand and supply and assess technical feasibility. The ability to evaluate the economic feasibility of material and energy strategies. The ability to account for social and environmental externalities. The ability to develop holistic value proposition. The ability to identify activities for value creation, capture, and delivery. The ability to develop core objectives. The ability to understand value created, value destroyed, value missed. CQ-CE-5-1, CQ-CE-5-2, CQ-CE-5-3, CQ-CE-5-4, CQ-CE-5-5, CQ-CE-5-6, CQ-CE-5-7, CQ-CE-5-8, CQ-CE-5-9, CQ-CE-5-10, CQ-CE-5-11, CQ-CE-5-12, CQ-CE-5-13, CQ-CE-5-14, CQ-CE-5-15, CQ-CE-5-16, CQ-CE-5-17, CQ-CE-5-18, CQ-CE-5-19 Quality can be dervied from a set of characteristics relevant for a certain use case. Objectives and value propositions may be implicit. CE6 The capacity to understand system barriers and external factors. Implementation actions: The ability to consider macro level energy infrastructure and legislature. CQ-CE-6-1, CQ-CE-6-2, CQ-CE-6-3 CE7 The capacity to understand the effect of (a set of) actions (on the system). Implementation actions: The ability to understand success factors of exchanges. The ability to measure and compare material flows. The ability to evaluate direct and indirect effects. The ability to evaluate energy consumption and carbon emissions. The ability to analyse large amount of data fast. The ability to manage the dynamic and complexity of energy data. The ability to measure rebound effects. The ability to establish (prompt) feedback structures. The ability to measure economic, environmental and social value each. The ability to combine all dimensions of value for a comprehensive evaluation. The ability to assess value created, missed, destroyed. CQ-CE-7-1, CQ-CE-7-2, CQ-CE-7-3, CQ-CE-7-4, CQ-CE-7-5, CQ-CE-7-6, CQ-CE-7-7, CQ-CE-7-8 Most effects are not explicit but derivable from direct outputs and inputs of processes.

docs.title: Circular Enabler Category 3 – The capacity to adapt
docs.text: This caregory includes 2 specific stories and 8 CQs.

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text: ID Description CQs Reasoning Requirements CE8 The capacity to acquire and share (new) knowledge. Implementation actions:  The ability to track actions and decisions made by system actors. The ability to collect data on energy during all life cycle phases. The ability to incentivize the sharing of data. CQ-CE-8-1, CQ-CE-8-2, CQ-CE-8-3 Energy consumption/surplus may be derivable based on detailed data, if available. Incentives may be implicit. CE9 The capacity to develop new configurations. Implementation actions: The ability to understand the qualities and characteristics of a material. The ability to collect and process dynamic and complex energy data quickly. The ability to simulate processes to identify efficiency potential. The ability to define different types of value. The ability to understand underlying needs and wants. CQ-CE-9-1, CQ-CE-9-2, CQ-CE-9-3, CQ-CE-9-4, CQ-CE-9-5 Quality of a material in relation to a certain need may be derived from its characteristics. Efficiency of a process might be derivable from the potential outputs given an ideal input and processing situation. Values and needs may be implicit.

docs.title: Circular Enabler Category 4 – The capacity of actors to collaborate
docs.text: This caregory includes 2 specific stories and 21 CQs.

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text: ID Description CQs Reasoning Requirements CE10 The capacity to work together for a shared goal. Implementation actions: The ability to share infrastructure (Hardware and software) for material and energy flows. The ability to align processes. The ability to collaborate for energy recovery. The ability to bring together all energy sector stakeholders. The ability to share information on energy demand and surplus. The ability to collaborate for value (co)creation, value transfer and value capture. CQ-CE-10-1, CQ-CE-10-2, CQ-CE-10-3, CQ-CE-10-4, CQ-CE-10-5, CQ-CE-10-6, CQ-CE-10-7, CQ-CE-10-8, CQ-CE-10-9, CQ-CE-10-10 Unused capacity, energy surplus and demand, process alignments, energy recovery potential may be derivable depending of the data available. CE11 The capacity to integrate (relevant) actors throughout entire process. Implementation actions: The ability to incentivize cooperation (on material flows). The ability to establish reciprocal information exchange (on material flows). The ability to allow and encourage active engagement by users (i.e., prosumers) in energy flows. The ability to collect and provide consumption data during use phase. The ability to include stakeholders during identification of value. The ability to integrate stakeholders in (value) evaluation processes. CQ-CE-11-1, CQ-CE-11-2, CQ-CE-11-3, CQ-CE-11-4, CQ-CE-11-5, CQ-CE-11-6, CQ-CE-11-7, CQ-CE-11-8, CQ-CE-11-9, CQ-CE-11-10, CQ-CE-11-11

docs.title: Circular Enabler Category 4 – The capacity to manage the system
docs.text: This caregory includes 2 specific stories and 17 CQs.

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text: ID Description CQs Reasoning Requirements CE12 The capacity to coordinate processes and actors for the benefit of the system. Implementation actions: The ability to manage risk in case of exchange failure (in material flows). The ability to manage energy exchanges decentralised. The ability to make decisions (on energy flows) automatically. The ability to establish shared vision (on value) and align objectives. The ability to ensure that responsibilities and obligations are met (to create value). CQ-CE-12-1, CQ-CE-12-2, CQ-CE-12-3, CQ-CE-12-4, CQ-CE-12-5, CQ-CE-12-6, CQ-CE-12-7, CQ-CE-12-8, CQ-CE-12-9, CQ-CE-12-10, CQ-CE-12-11 A CVN may not be centrally coordinated or governed by a formal agreement, hence, most information on responsibilities, objectives etc may only be implicitly derivable from other data. Contingency plans/options and best options may be derivable from descriptions of the needs and characteristics of processes. CE13 The capacity to interact and share information with actors in an effective and trustful way. Implementation actions: The ability to share information transparently and traceably. The ability to standardise material information. The ability to verify value creation. CQ-CE-13-1, CQ-CE-13-2, CQ-CE-13-3, CQ-CE-13-4, CQ-CE-13-5, CQ-CE-13-6 The responsibilities, history, access rights, verification status etc can be inherited from a larger collection of information, e.g. a document or data sheet, so the statements contained in it. Unless something is stated to be verified, it is treated as not verified.

docs.title: SPARQL Queries
docs.text: This section provides ready-to-use SPARQL queries for CEON. Each query corresponds to one or more Competency Questions and can be run against any SPARQL endpoint loaded with CEON data.

docs.title: Prefixes
docs.text:
All queries in this documentation use the following common prefixes:

PREFIX actorODP:   <https://w3id.org/CEON/ontology/actorODP/>
PREFIX actor:   <https://w3id.org/CEON/ontology/actor/>
PREFIX cvn:     <https://w3id.org/CEON/ontology/cvn/>
PREFIX value:   <https://w3id.org/CEON/ontology/value/>
PREFIX processODP: <https://w3id.org/CEON/ontology/processODP/>
PREFIX process: <https://w3id.org/CEON/ontology/process/>
PREFIX plan: <https://w3id.org/CEON/ontology/plan/>
PREFIX resourceODP: <https://w3id.org/CEON/ontology/resourceODP/>

PREFIX product: <https://w3id.org/CEON/ontology/product/>
PREFIX material:     <https://w3id.org/CEON/ontology/material/>
PREFIX energy: <https://w3id.org/CEON/ontology/energy/>
PREFIX owl:          <http://www.w3.org/2002/07/owl#>
PREFIX rdf:          <http://www.w3.org/1999/02/22-rdf-syntax-ns#>
PREFIX rdfs:         <http://www.w3.org/2000/01/rdf-schema#>
PREFIX xsd:          <http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema#>

docs.title: Circular Enablers Category
docs.text:
• Circular Enabler Category 1
  • Circular Enabler Category 2
  • Circular Enabler Category 3
  • Circular Enabler Category 4
  • Circular Enabler Category 5

docs.title: Circular Enabler Category 1 – SPARQL Queries
docs.text: All queries assume the common prefixes are declared.

docs.title: CQ-CE-1-1
docs.text:
What are the connections and dependencies between actors and processes in a certain value network?

SELECT ?actor ?role
WHERE{
    ?actor rdf:type actorODP:Actor .
    ?participation rdf:type actorODP:Participation .
    ?participation actorODP:participatingActor ?actor .
    ?participation actorODP:participantRole ?role .
    ?role rdf:type actorODP:Role .
    ?participation actorODP:participationIn cvn:Colloboration .
}

docs.title: CQ-CE-1-2
docs.text:
What is the energy components in this system, e.g. exergy and anergy?

SELECT ?component ?exergy ?anergy
WHERE{
    ?component energy:hasExergy ?exergy .
    ?component energy:hasAnergy ?anergy .
}

docs.title: CQ-CE-1-3
docs.text:
What are the involved value forms, e.g. economic, social, environmental?

SELECT ?value
WHERE{
    ?cvn rdf:type cvn:CVN .
    ?cvn cvn:createsValue ?value .
    ?value rdf:type value:Value .
}

docs.title: CQ-CE-2-1
docs.text:
What are the actors (and their roles) in the value network?

  • Same as: CQ-CE-1-1.sparql

docs.title: CQ-CE-2-2
docs.text:
What are the connections and dependencies between actors and materials/components/products in a certain value network?

  • Same as: CQ-CE-1-1.sparql

docs.title: CQ-CE-3-1
docs.text:
What is the value network implementing in terms of circular strategies?

SELECT ?strategy
WHERE{
    ?cvn rdf:type cvn:CVN .
    ?cvn cvn:implementsStrategy ?strategy .
    ?strategy rdf:type cvn:CircularStrategy .
}

docs.title: CQ-CE-3-2
docs.text:
What is the process breakdown of this life cycle (what processes are involved and in what order), across actors?

SELECT ?process ?prevproc ?nextproc
WHERE {
    ?process rdf:type process:TransformationProcess .
    OPTIONAL {
        ?nextproc rdf:type process:TransformationProcess .
        ?prevproc processODP:nextProcess ?nextproc . 
    }
    OPTIONAL {
        ?prevproc rdf:type processODP:Transformation .
        ?prevproc processODP:nextProcess ?process .
    }
    ?participation actorODP:participationIn ?process .
    ?participation actorODP:participatingActor ?actor.
}

docs.title: CQ-CE-3-3
docs.text:
What is the status and location of this material at this point in time?

SELECT ?resource ?location
WHERE {
    ?resource rdf:type resourceODP:Resource .
    ?resource location:hasLocation ?Location . 
}

docs.title: CQ-CE-3-4
docs.text:
What are the elements (e.g. steps/work and actors) involved in this process?

SELECT ?participation ?component 
WHERE{
    ?process rdf:type processODP:Process .
    ?participation actorODP:participationIn ?process .
    { ?participation actorODP:participatingActor ?component }
    UNION
    { ?participation actorODP:participatingResource ?component }
}

docs.title: CQ-CE-3-5
docs.text:
What is the status and location of this material at this point in time?

SELECT ?process ?input ?output 
WHERE{
    ?process rdf:type processODP:Process .
    { ?process processODP:hasInput ?input }
    UNION
    { ?process processODP:hasOutput ?output }
}

docs.title: CQ-CE-4-1
docs.text:
What are the related circular strategies of this and other related value networks?

SELECT ?cvn ?strategy
WHERE{
    ?cvn rdf:type cvn:CVN .
    ?cvn cvn:composedOf ?cvn .
    ?strategy rdf:type cvn:CircularStrategy .
    ?cvn cvn:implementsStrategy ?strategy .
}

docs.title: CQ-CE-4-2
docs.text:
What are the connections and dependencies between actors and materials/components/products in a certain value network?

  • Same as: CQ-CE-1-1.sparql

docs.title: CQ-CE-4-3
docs.text:
What is the carbon intensity and other sustainability factors of this energy source?

SELECT ?source ?carbonIntensity ?sustainability
WHERE {
  ?energy rdf:type energy:Energy .
  ?energy energy:hasEnergySource ?source .
  { ?source energy:hashasCarbonIntensity ?carbonIntensity }
  UNION
  { ?source energy:Sustainability ?sustainability }
}

docs.title: CQ-CE-4-4
docs.text:
What are the elements (e.g. steps/work and actors) involved in this process?

  • Same as: CQ-CE-3-4.sparql

docs.title: Circular Enabler Category 2 – SPARQL Queries
docs.text: All queries assume the common prefixes are declared.

docs.title: CQ-CE-5-1
docs.text: What are the barriers or missing actors/processes/resources to implement a resource exchange/material flow?

docs.title: CQ-CE-5-2
docs.text:
What is the material breakdown of a product or component?

SELECT ?physicalObject ?component
WHERE {
    ?physicalObject rdf:type resourceODP:PhysicalObject .
    ?physicalObject resource:hasMatter ?material .
    ?material material:hasMaterialComponent ?component .
}

docs.title: CQ-CE-5-3
docs.text: In what units of measure are values expressed?

docs.title: CQ-CE-5-4
docs.text: What is the quality of a flow at a specific time?

docs.title: CQ-CE-5-5
docs.text: What is the quantity of a flow at a specific time?

docs.title: CQ-CE-5-6
docs.text: From what source does this data originate?

docs.title: CQ-CE-5-7
docs.text:
What are the elements (e.g. steps/work and actors) involved in this process?

  • Same as: CQ-CE-3-4.sparql

docs.title: CQ-CE-5-8
docs.text: What is the origin and complete trace of this material?

docs.title: CQ-CE-5-9
docs.text:
What is the overall energy consumption to produce this material/component/product?

SELECT ?execution ?energy
WHERE {
    ?execution processODP:isSettingFor ?process.
    ?energy rdf:type process:Energy .
    ?process process:needsEnergy ?energy .
}

docs.title: CQ-CE-5-10
docs.text: What are the rebound effects and added energy requirements of a material flow?

docs.title: CQ-CE-5-11
docs.text: What are the alternatives to this flow?

docs.title: CQ-CE-5-12
docs.text:
What are the energy demands of this process?

SELECT ?energy
WHERE {
    ?energy rdf:type process:Energy .
    ?process process:needsEnergy ?energy .
}

docs.title: CQ-CE-5-13
docs.text:
What are the technical and/or economic requirements of implementing this process/strategy?

  • Same as: CQ-CE-3-1.sparql

docs.title: CQ-CE-5-14
docs.text: What effects would it have on external social and environmental factors?

docs.title: CQ-CE-5-15
docs.text:
What are the needs in terms of social and environmental factors?

SELECT ?capability ?resourcerelation
WHERE {
    ?capability rdf:type actorODP:Capability .
    ?capability actor:neededResourceRelation ?resourcerelation . 
}

docs.title: CQ-CE-5-16
docs.text:
What is the value proposition of the overall value network and life cycle?

SELECT ?cvn ?valueProposition
WHERE {
    ?cvn rdf:type cvn:CVN .
    ?cvn cvn:aimsAtValue ?valuePropostition .
    ?valueProposition rdf:type value:ValueProposition .
}

docs.title: CQ-CE-5-17
docs.text:
What are the activities related to this value creation, capture and delivery?

SELECT ?process ?perception
WHERE{
    ?perception rdf:type value:ValuePerception .
    ?perception value:onValueParticipation ?participation .
    ?proposition value:targetingParticipation ?participation .
    ?cvn cvn:aimsAtValue ?proposition .
    ?cvn cvn:composedOf ?process .
}

docs.title: CQ-CE-5-18
docs.text:
What are the objectives of this value network/actor/process?

SELECT ?cvn ?valueProposition
WHERE{
    ?cvn rdf:type cvn:CVN .
    ?cvn cvn:aimsAtValue ?valueProposition .
    ?cvn cvn:composedOf ?process .
}

docs.title: CQ-CE-5-19
docs.text:
What is the value created/missed/destroyed by this flow/process?

SELECT ?process ?value
WHERE{
    ?process cvn:createsValue ?value .
    ?value rdf:type value:Value .
}

docs.title: CQ-CE-6-1
docs.text: What external factors affect this process/actor/value network?

docs.title: CQ-CE-6-2
docs.text: What is the legal conditions and legislation affecting this actor/object/process?

docs.title: CQ-CE-6-3
docs.text:
What is the energy infrastructure available?

SELECT ?infra
WHERE{
    ?infra rdf:type energy:EnergyInfrastruture .
}

docs.title: CQ-CE-7-1
docs.text:
What were the methods/algorithms used to measure or assess a certain value or decision, and what input data was used?

SELECT ?s ?p ?o WHERE {?s ?p ?o} LIMIT 10

docs.title: CQ-CE-7-2
docs.text:
What are the direct/indirect effects/outcomes of an action?

SELECT ?s ?p ?o WHERE {?s ?p ?o} LIMIT 10

docs.title: CQ-CE-7-3
docs.text: Was the (material, information) exchange successful?

docs.title: CQ-CE-7-4
docs.text:
What is the energy consumption of an action/process?

  • Same as: CQ-CE-5-12.sparql

docs.title: CQ-CE-7-5
docs.text:
What is the carbon emission of an action/process?

SELECT ?process ?CO2
WHERE {
    ?process process:producesCO2 ?CO2 .
}

docs.title: CQ-CE-7-6
docs.text:
At what time did this energy data change, and what is its detailed breakdown?

SELECT ?energyComposition ?percentage
WHERE {
    ?energyComposition actorODP:participatingObject energy:Energy .
    ?energyComposition energy:hasEnergyComponentPercentage ?percentage .
}

docs.title: CQ-CE-7-7
docs.text: What are the potential rebound effects of this action/process?

docs.title: CQ-CE-7-8
docs.text:
What is the economic, environmental and social value created/missed/destroyed by a process?

  • Same as: CQ-CE-5-19.sparql

docs.title: Circular Enabler Category 3 – SPARQL Queries
docs.text: All queries assume the common prefixes are declared.

docs.title: CQ-CE-8-1
docs.text:
What are the actions/decisions made by a certain actor at a certain point in time, in relation to a certain collaboration/process/material etc.?

SELECT ?s ?p ?o WHERE {?s ?p ?o} LIMIT 10

docs.title: CQ-CE-8-2
docs.text:
What is the energy input/usage/surplus during a certain life cycle phase of a material/component/product?

SELECT ?process ?energy
WHERE {
    ?energy rdf:type process:Energy .
    ?process process:needsEnergy ?energy .
}

docs.title: CQ-CE-8-3
docs.text:
Why should an actor share certain data?

SELECT ?s ?p ?o WHERE {?s ?p ?o} LIMIT 10

docs.title: CQ-CE-9-1
docs.text:
What are the characteristics, including quality, of this material?

SELECT ?resource ?quality
WHERE {
    ?resource rdf:type resourceODP:Resource .
    ?resource resourceODP:hasResourceQuality ?quality .
}

docs.title: CQ-CE-9-2
docs.text:
What is the data of this energy flow at this point in time?

SELECT ?s ?p ?o WHERE {?s ?p ?o} LIMIT 10

docs.title: CQ-CE-9-3
docs.text: How efficient is this process?

docs.title: CQ-CE-9-4
docs.text:
What kinds of value are involved in this collaboration/process?

  • Same as: CQ-CE-5-16.sparql

docs.title: CQ-CE-9-5
docs.text:
What are the needs underlying this value/collaboration/process?

SELECT ?resource
WHERE{
    ?process processODP:hasInput ?resource .
}

docs.title: Circular Enabler Category 4 – SPARQL Queries
docs.text: All queries assume the common prefixes are declared.

docs.title: CQ-CE-10-1
docs.text:
What capacity (e.g. competence) is needed from actors in order to collaborate?

SELECT ?capability ?actor
WHERE{
    ?capability actorODP:capabilityOf ?actor .
}

docs.title: CQ-CE-10-2
docs.text: What information is needed to collaborate and align processes?

docs.title: CQ-CE-10-3
docs.text: What material or energy flow uses a certain (type of) infrastructure?

docs.title: CQ-CE-10-4
docs.text:
What is the infrastructure available to, or held by, a certain actor?

SELECT ?actor ?infra
WHERE{
    ?participation actorODP:participatingInfrastructure ?infra .
    ?participation actorODP:participatingActor ?actor .
}

docs.title: CQ-CE-10-5
docs.text: What capacity of infrastructure is unused and can be shared with others?

docs.title: CQ-CE-10-6
docs.text:
What are the alignments between processes across actors, e.g. matching inputs and outputs, time etc?

SELECT ?s ?p ?o WHERE {?s ?p ?o} LIMIT 10

docs.title: CQ-CE-10-7
docs.text:
What are the energy outputs of an actor/process?

SELECT ?process ?energy
WHERE {
    ?energy rdf:type process:Energy .
    ?process processODP:hasOutput ?energy .
}

docs.title: CQ-CE-10-8
docs.text: What is the potential for energy recovery?

docs.title: CQ-CE-10-9
docs.text: What energy surplus/demand does an actor have?

docs.title: CQ-CE-10-10
docs.text:
What value can be (co)created/transferred/captured by and actor or a collaboration?

  • Same as: CQ-CE-5-17.sparql

docs.title: CQ-CE-11-1
docs.text: What capacity (e.g. competence) is needed to ingrate an acor in the network?

docs.title: CQ-CE-11-2
docs.text:
What is the value created (incentive) for a certain actor in a certain collaboration/process?

  • Same as: CQ-CE-5-17.sparql

docs.title: CQ-CE-11-3
docs.text:
What information does a certain actor hold, or have access to?

SELECT ?resource
WHERE{
    ?resourceRelation actorODP:participatingResource ?resource .
    ?resourceRelation actorODP:participatingActor ?actor .
}

docs.title: CQ-CE-11-4
docs.text: What is the access control information for this piece of information/who do I share the information with?

docs.title: CQ-CE-11-5
docs.text:
For what is this information needed/used?

SELECT ?process ?information
WHERE{
    ?process processODP:hasInput ?information .
}

docs.title: CQ-CE-11-6
docs.text: Who and hos is energy contributed to this process?

docs.title: CQ-CE-11-7
docs.text:
Who is consuming/using this product?

SELECT ?process ?actor ?product
WHERE{
    ?process processODP:hasInput ?product.
    ?collaboration actorODP:participationIn ?process.
    ?collaboration actorODP:participatingActor ?actor.
}

docs.title: CQ-CE-11-8
docs.text: How is the product used?

docs.title: CQ-CE-11-9
docs.text: How was usage data collected?

docs.title: CQ-CE-11-10
docs.text:
What is the potential/observed/confimed value of this material/component/product or process/action/collaboration to a certain actor?

  • Same as: CQ-CE-5-17.sparql

docs.title: CQ-CE-11-11
docs.text: Who evaluated the value and by what method?

docs.title: All queries assume the common prefixes are declared.
CQ-CE-12-1
docs.text:
Who/what actor is responsible for what part of the processes?

SELECT ?role ?actor ?process
WHERE{
    ?participation actorODP:participantRole ?role.
    ?participation actorODP:participatingActor ?actor.
    ?participation actorODP:participationIn ?process.
}

docs.title: CQ-CE-12-2
docs.text:
What capabillity (e.g. competence) is needed from an actor?

  • Same as: CQ-CE-10-1.sparql

docs.title: CQ-CE-12-3
docs.text: What is the contingency plan/other possibilities for replacing a material flow in a process?

docs.title: CQ-CE-12-4
docs.text:
Where does this material/component/product come from (source, supplier)?

SELECT ?s ?p ?o WHERE {?s ?p ?o} LIMIT 10

docs.title: CQ-CE-12-5
docs.text: Where does the energy needed for a certain process come from (source, supplier)?

docs.title: CQ-CE-12-6
docs.text: What is the contingency plan/other options for replacing that source of energy?

docs.title: CQ-CE-12-7
docs.text: What is the best option for energy replacement in this case?

docs.title: CQ-CE-12-8
docs.text:
What is the objective/vision of this circular value network?

  • Same as: CQ-CE-5-17.sparql

docs.title: CQ-CE-12-9
docs.text: What are the objectives of the actors involved in the network and how are they related to the overall objective/vision?

docs.title: CQ-CE-12-10
docs.text:
What are the obligations and responsibilities of this actor in this collaboration (e.g. circular value network)?

SELECT ?role ?collaboration
WHERE{
    ?participation actorODP:participantRole ?role.
    ?participation actorODP:participatingActor ?actor.
    ?participation actorODP:participationIn ?collaboration.
}

docs.title: CQ-CE-12-11
docs.text: Have the obligations and responsibilities been met?

docs.title: CQ-CE-13-1
docs.text: Who/what actor is responsible for this information?

docs.title: CQ-CE-13-2
docs.text: What is the history of changes of this information?

docs.title: CQ-CE-13-3
docs.text: Who has access to this information?

docs.title: CQ-CE-13-4
docs.text: Who has accessed this information?

docs.title: CQ-CE-13-5
docs.text: What standard/metadata stardard/format is used for the material data?

docs.title: CQ-CE-13-6
docs.text: How was this created value verified/is it verified?

docs.title: Usage Examples
docs.text:
This section provides end-to-end examples of how to use CEON to represent real-world data in the three use case domains of the Onto-DESIDE project.

Each example includes:

  • A description of the scenario
  • Sample data in Turtle format
  • SPARQL queries to retrieve information from the data

docs.title: Use Cases
docs.text:
• Construction — material passports and building component lifecycles
  • Electronics — product traceability and end-of-life processing
  • Textile — fibre composition and recycling value chains

docs.title: Ontology Maintenance Plan
docs.text: This section documents the maintenance plan for the Circular Economy Ontology Network (CEON) and the three use case ontologies developed within the Onto-DESIDE project (Deliverable D3.7).

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Although the Onto-DESIDE project ended in November 2025, the developed ontologies are intended to support further applications where knowledge representation of the Circular Economy domain is required. The maintenance plan ensures CEON remains adaptable, interoperable, and usable over time.

The plan is grounded in the ontology evolution process outlined by Zablith et al., which consists of five essential steps:

Step Description Detecting the Need for Evolution Identifying new requirements the ontology should capture Suggesting Changes Conceptualizing, formalizing, and implementing changes Validating Changes Verifying consistency and constraint satisfaction Assessing Impact Measuring impact on artifacts dependent on the ontology Managing Changes Applying, documenting, and version-controlling changes

docs.title: Community & Standardization
docs.text: The ontology maintenance strategy is guided not only by methodological and technical considerations but also by community engagement and collaborative standardization practices.

docs.title: W3C Community Group for CE and DPPs
docs.text:
Onto-DESIDE established a W3C Community Group focused on Circular Economy and Digital Product Passports (DPPs), with the group identifier ce-dpp.

W3C Community Groups are informal open forums within the World Wide Web Consortium where participants share and discuss ideas related to web technologies. They serve as a first step towards formal technical web standards.

docs.title: Mission
docs.text:
The group's mission is to support the technical implementation of infrastructure and applications for the Circular Economy and Digital Product Passports. Key activities include:

  • Discussing requirements set by CE and DPPs on web technologies and standards
  • Identifying standardization challenges not yet addressed
  • Sharing experiences and ideas on usage of web technologies in CE
  • Complementing the work of ISO/TC 323 on CE standardization from a technical web perspective

docs.title: Ongoing discussion topics
docs.text:
• Practical applications of the developed ontologies and their standardization needs
  • Feedback on challenges in implementation and use of existing web standards in CE and DPP
  • Data models specific for CE and DPP
  • Best practices in data sharing
  • Ontology integration across platforms and domains

Join the group

The W3C Community Group is open to all parties interested in the technical aspects of CE and DPP implementation. Join here

docs.title: RDA FAIR Mappings Working Group
docs.text: To ensure that ontology alignments are as interoperable and reusable as possible, Onto-DESIDE joined the FAIR Mappings Working Group at the Research Data Alliance (RDA).

docs.title: Objectives
docs.text: The working group collaborates with various communities to develop common guidelines making ontology mappings FAIR — Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, and Reusable.

docs.title: CEON contribution
docs.text:
CEON-related mappings were proposed as a case study within the working group, providing example mappings to support the group's goals. This complements alignment work at other venues such as the CE track at OAEI.

More information:

  • RDA FAIR Mappings WG
  • CEON case study

docs.title: Maintenance Scenarios
docs.text: Three scenarios are anticipated in which maintenance of CEON and the use case ontologies will be necessary.

docs.title: Scenario 1 — Reuse and Extension
docs.text:
CEON or the use case ontologies may be reused in future initiatives for purposes such as knowledge representation, data documentation, and standardization within the circular economy domain. This reuse could involve:

  • Extending CEON's existing module structure
  • Aligning with other ontologies through existing or new alignments provided by Onto-DESIDE

Primary focus

Maintenance in this scenario focuses on adding specialisations of existing modules, ensuring comprehensive documentation, and providing usage examples — rather than implementing substantial structural changes.

docs.title: Scenario 2 — New or Evolving Requirements
docs.text:
New domain requirements may arise or existing ones may evolve, necessitating updates to the content or structure of CEON. Maintenance in this context should:

  • Adhere to established ontology evolution and engineering methodologies
  • Ensure changes are systematically validated, implemented, and documented

See Methodological Support for the recommended approaches.

docs.title: Scenario 3 — Bug Fixes
docs.text:
Bugs or issues detected during usage of CEON should be resolved through the standard GitHub issue and pull request workflow described in Technical Support.

Reporting issues

Issues can be reported directly on the CEON GitHub repository.

docs.title: Applicability
docs.text:
All three scenarios apply equally to:

  • The CEON core modules
  • The three use case ontologies: Textile, Electronics, and Construction

docs.title: Ontology Development Methodology
docs.text: The development of CEON and the use case ontologies has followed a variant of the eXtreme Design (XD) methodology combined with Modular Ontology Modeling (MOMo). This same methodology should be followed in future maintenance activities. For full details, see the Methodology page.

docs.title: Application to maintenance scenarios
docs.text: Scenario Recommended approach Scenario 1 Follow XD/MOMo methodology to extend existing modules or create new specializations Scenario 2 Follow XD/MOMo methodology to update content or structure based on new requirements Scenario 3 Use GitHub issue tracking and pull requests

docs.title: Ontology Alignment at OAEI
docs.text:
Ontology alignment is an essential activity in maintenance, particularly for Scenario 1 involving reuse and extension. The Onto-DESIDE project established a pipeline for ontology alignment covering three key tasks:

  • Task a — aligning CE-specific ontologies with each other
  • Task b — aligning CEON with industry domain-specific ontologies
  • Task c — aligning CEON with top-level ontologies

The pipeline consists of five steps:

flowchart LR
    A[Matching by
OM Tools] --> B[Voting or
Filtering]
    B --> C[Validation &
Manual Matching]
    C --> D[Conflict
Checking]
    D --> E[Publishing &
Maintaining
Alignments]

Onto-DESIDE also created a dedicated CE track at OAEI in 2024, enabling systematic assessment of how well CEON aligns with other CE-related ontologies. More information: OAEI 2024 CE track

docs.title: CE-related Ontology Catalog
docs.text:
To support ongoing alignment and interoperability efforts, the Onto-DESIDE project compiled a catalog of CE-related ontologies across six focus domains:

Domain Topics Circular Economy Business models, resource recovery, waste, recycling, circularity assessment Sustainability Sustainability goals, performance, environment, energy Materials Raw materials, material composition Logistics Distribution, production, supply chain Manufacturing Manufacturing processes Products Product lifecycle

The catalog will continue to be maintained by regularly monitoring prefix.cc and LOV — two ontology registries where CEON is already registered.

Full catalog: liusemweb.github.io/Circular-Economy-Ontology-Catalogue

docs.title: Ontology Development via GitHub
docs.text: The CEON GitHub repository is the central platform for ontology development, versioning, and publication. The collaborative workflow established during Onto-DESIDE will continue to support future maintenance.

docs.title: Workflow
docs.text:
flowchart LR
    A[Create feature
branch] --> B[Develop &
commit changes]
    B --> C[Open Pull
Request]
    C --> D[Review &
discussion]
    D --> E[Merge to
main branch]
    E --> F[Auto-deploy
via GitHub Actions]

docs.title: Key practices
docs.text:
• Branching — development happens on feature branches, keeping the main branch stable
  • Commit messages — clear, descriptive messages document the history of progress
  • Pull Requests — formal proposals to merge changes, including summaries, diffs, and links to related issues
  • Issues — used to track bugs, tasks, and feature requests; can be assigned, labelled, and linked to PRs
  • Releases — new versions are published using GitHub's release tools, providing clear milestones and version history

docs.title: Developer guidelines
docs.text: Full developer guidelines are documented in the CEON README.

docs.title: CE-related Ontology Catalog
docs.text: To support ongoing alignment and interoperability efforts, the Onto-DESIDE project compiled and documented a catalog of CE-related ontologies identified through a comprehensive literature study.

docs.title: Focus domains
docs.text: Domain Topics Circular Economy Business models, resource recovery, waste, recycling, circularity assessment Sustainability Sustainability goals, performance, environment, energy Materials Raw materials, material composition Logistics Distribution, production, supply chain Manufacturing Manufacturing processes Products Product lifecycle

docs.title: Ongoing monitoring
docs.text:
The catalog will continue to be maintained beyond the Onto-DESIDE project by regularly monitoring:

  • prefix.cc — ontology prefix registry where CEON is registered
  • LOV (Linked Open Vocabularies) — ontology registry where CEON is registered

The full catalog is available at: liusemweb.github.io/Circular-Economy-Ontology-Catalogue

docs.title: Development Process
docs.text: This section describes how CEON was developed — the methodology, design decisions, and tooling used to create and maintain the ontology network.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
CEON follows the eXtreme Design (XD) modular ontology engineering approach, where each relevant topic (actors, processes, resources, etc.) is developed as a separate ontology module that can be independently reused.

The development process is iterative in three rounds and driven by:

  1. Domain requirements collected from use case partners in the Onto-DESIDE project
  2. Competency questions that the ontology must be able to answer
  3. Ontology Design Patterns (ODPs) that provide reusable building blocks
  4. Ontology Evaluation that identify issues in the development
  5. Alignment with existing ontologies to improve semantic interoperability

docs.title: Ontology Development and Publishing
docs.text:
The XD methodology was slightly adapted for the Onto-DESIDE project context. Specifically, the XD principle of pair design was modified — rather than requiring ontology engineers to work continuously in pairs, a review-based approach was adopted instead:

  1. An ontology module is created by one ontology engineer
  2. It is then reviewed by another ontology engineer

This approach is in line with code review practices in software engineering, ensuring quality without requiring continuous synchronization.

docs.title: Development pipeline
docs.text:
flowchart LR
    A[Identify\nRequirements & CQs] --> B[Select\nODPs]
    B --> C[Create Module\nDiagram]
    C --> D[Implement\nin OWL]
    D --> E[Review by\nanother engineer]
    E --> F{Pass?}
    F -- No --> D
    F -- Yes --> G[Publish via\nGitHub]

docs.title: Ontology Deployment via GitHub
docs.text:
The CEON GitHub repository is the central platform for ontology development, versioning, and publication. The collaborative workflow established during Onto-DESIDE will continue to support future maintenance.

flowchart LR
    A[Create feature
branch] --> B[Develop &
commit changes]
    B --> C[Open Pull
Request]
    C --> D[Review &
discussion]
    D --> E[Merge to
main branch]
    E --> F[Auto-deploy
via GitHub Actions]

Key practices:

  • Branching — development happens on feature branches, keeping main stable
  • Commit messages — clear, descriptive messages document the history of progress
  • Pull Requests — formal proposals to merge changes, including summaries, diffs, and links to related issues
  • Issues — used to track bugs, tasks, and feature requests
  • Releases — new versions published using GitHub's release tools

Full developer guidelines are in the CEON README.

docs.title: Versioning and publishing
docs.text:
The development of the ontology network entailed multiple interdependent ontologies, several of which went through multiple development iterations. To manage this:

  • A GitHub repository is used for versioning and creating new releases
  • The w3id service provides permanent identifiers under a common namespace at http://w3id.org/CEON/

docs.title: Methodology
docs.text:
CEON is developed using a combination of two agile ontology engineering methodologies: eXtreme Design (XD) and Modular Ontology Modeling (MOMo). This combination was chosen based on an analysis of the Onto-DESIDE project requirements and structure.

The combination includes:

  • Adopting rapid prototyping and a requirements-driven approach from XD
  • Following the overall outline from MOMo, including identifying existing Ontology Design Patterns (ODPs) and creating module diagrams

docs.title: Why This Combination?
docs.text:
The Onto-DESIDE project had three evaluation iterations, making an agile methodology a natural fit — one that prioritizes flexibility, domain expert requirements, and rapid iterative delivery. Furthermore, the project requirements highlighted the need for cross-domain modeling spanning topics such as resources, processes, circular value networks, and dedicated industry use cases (construction, electronics, and textile). An ontology network with general ODPs addresses cross-domain interoperability while maintaining suitable modeling granularity.

Other methodologies such as LOT (Linked Open Terms) and SAMOD were considered. XD was selected as the most suitable since it combines agile development principles with the use of ODPs. Although LOT was not explicitly followed, an ontology development and publishing pipeline was set up to satisfy the FAIR principles (Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, and Reusable), making the work generally aligned with LOT as well.

docs.title: Requirements Analysis
docs.text:
The Onto-DESIDE project requirements span multiple perspectives:

Top-down (CE Requirements)Bottom-up (Use Case Requirements)

13 CE requirements (circular enablers) were developed based on general CE terminology and concepts used in standards and value flow analysis methodologies such as Circularity Thinking. These represent cross-domain challenges and circularity concepts.

44 use-case requirements were developed across the three industry domains:

Domain Requirements Construction 13 Electronics 6 Textile 23

The requirements were then mapped to 9 topics:

  • Circular value network
  • Value
  • Actor
  • Process
  • Material
  • Product
  • Location
  • Quantities and units
  • Provenance

Based on these topics, relevant Competency Questions (CQs), contextual statements, and reasoning statements were proposed. The latest set of ontological requirements includes 90 CQs across 13 CE requirements. Use-case related CQs were also proposed for developing the use-case ontologies.

docs.title: Example CE Requirements and CQs
docs.text: CE Requirement Competency Questions The capacity to understand interrelations between processes and actors in the system What are the connections and dependencies between actors and processes in a certain value network? What are the energy components in this system, e.g., exergy and anergy? The capacity to identify and consider all (relevant) system actors What are the actors (and their roles) in the value network? What are the connections and dependencies between actors and materials/components/products in a certain value network? The capacity to consider processes throughout entire life cycle What is the value network implementing in terms of circular strategies? What is the process breakdown of this life cycle? The capacity to develop new configurations What are the characteristics, including quality, of this material? What kinds of value are involved in this collaboration/process?
